narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know receiving a rejection from law school is disheartening, especially when it's based on a prediction about your future bar exam performance, and I want to help you understand exactly why this happened in a straightforward way. Your profile includes being a Black male from an upper-middle-income family, planning to attend full-time, with an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 34, a Year 1 school decile ranking of 5, and a notably low Year 3 decile ranking of 1. The model assessed your overall risk and predicted a 45% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which was enough to tip the decision toward rejection since the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass. This prediction wasn't driven by any single overwhelming factor but by a combination of your academic indicators, ranked by their influence as follows: your Year 1 decile had the strongest pull, followed closely by your Year 3 decile, then family income, LSAT score, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and finally gender. Each of the top factors—your Year 1 decile (slightly below the typical 5.71 average), Year 3 decile (far below the 5.53 average), family income bracket, LSAT (under the 36.70 average), racial background as Black (less common than the 83.8% White majority), and undergraduate GPA (below the 3.21 average)—pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, as they signal higher risk compared to students who typically pass. Your full-time enrollment status had a minor similar nudge, while being male (the more common 56.4% in the dataset) slightly favored a passing prediction, offering a small counterbalance. Overall, your Year 3 performance stood out as particularly influential because it was in the bottom decile school-wide, unlike most admitted students who rank much higher by that point. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your metrics across grades, test scores, and GPA were consistently lower, placing you in a riskier group despite your solid family background. To improve your chances moving forward, focus on what you can realistically strengthen: retake the LSAT to aim above 36.7 if possible, build stronger academic habits to boost future GPA equivalents in grad programs, and prepare intensively for the bar through targeted study or prep courses, as past deciles show grades are key but changeable with effort. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact areas, so this rejection is a chance to refine your path. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing.
